expanded_description: Addition reactions of alkenes involve the breaking of the carbon-carbon double bond ($\text{C}=\text{C}$) and the addition of atoms or groups across this bond. This section explores the three major types of alkene addition reactions: hydrogenation (addition of $\text{H}_2$), halogenation (addition of $\text{X}_2$), and hydrohalogenation (addition of $\text{HX}$). Understanding these reactions is fundamental to organic synthesis, as they convert unsaturated alkenes into saturated products with predictable regiochemistry and stereochemistry.

core_concept.principle: The $\pi$-Bond of Alkenes is Electron-Rich and Susceptible to Electrophilic Attack
core_concept.explanation: The carbon-carbon double bond consists of one strong $\sigma$-bond and one weaker $\pi$-bond. The $\pi$-bond electrons are more exposed and less tightly held than $\sigma$-bond electrons, making them nucleophilic (electron-rich). This electron density attracts electrophiles (electron-deficient species), initiating addition reactions. The $\pi$-bond breaks, and two new $\sigma$-bonds form, converting the $sp^2$ hybridized carbons to $sp^3$ hybridization. This transformation is energetically favorable because two strong $\sigma$-bonds replace one strong $\sigma$-bond and one weaker $\pi$-bond.

image.alt: Diagram showing alkene double bond breaking and atoms adding across it
image.caption: Addition reaction: $\pi$-bond breaks, two new $\sigma$-bonds form, converting $sp^2$ carbons to $sp^3$.

sections.section_title: What Are Addition Reactions of Alkenes?
sections.introduction: Addition reactions are the characteristic reactions of alkenes, where the $\pi$-bond is broken and new atoms or groups are added to the carbons that were previously double-bonded. Unlike substitution reactions (which replace atoms), addition reactions increase the number of atoms in the molecule. These reactions are fundamental to organic chemistry because they allow conversion of simple alkenes into a wide variety of functionalized products. The three most important addition reactions are hydrogenation (industrial food production), halogenation (synthetic intermediates), and hydrohalogenation (alkyl halide synthesis).

key_concept.definition: An addition reaction of an alkene is a chemical reaction in which the $\pi$-bond of the $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond breaks, and two new $\sigma$-bonds form as atoms or groups add to the two carbons, converting the alkene to a saturated product.
key_concept.context: Addition reactions are exothermic (release energy) because two strong $\sigma$-bonds form while only one weaker $\pi$-bond breaks. The general pattern is: $\text{C}=\text{C} + \text{A}-\text{B} \rightarrow \text{C}(\text{A})-\text{C}(\text{B})$. Regioselectivity (which carbon gets which group) and stereochemistry (spatial arrangement) are key considerations.

image.alt: General equation for alkene addition reaction
image.caption: General addition: $\text{R}_2\text{C}=\text{CR}_2 + \text{A}-\text{B} \rightarrow \text{R}_2\text{C}(\text{A})-\text{C}(\text{B})\text{R}_2$.

principles.statement: The alkene $\pi$-bond acts as a nucleophile (electron donor), attacking electrophiles to initiate addition reactions through electrophilic addition mechanism.
principles.application: This explains why alkenes react with electrophiles ($\text{H}^+$, $\text{Br}^+$, etc.) but not with nucleophiles. The $\pi$-electrons attack the electrophile first, forming a carbocation intermediate (or cyclic intermediate for halogens), which is then attacked by a nucleophile to complete the addition.
principles.analogy: Think of the $\pi$-bond as a 'magnet for positive charges': the electron-rich $\pi$-cloud attracts electrophiles like a magnet attracts iron. Once the electrophile is attracted, the $\pi$-bond 'grabs' it, breaking in the process.
principles.example: Ethene + $\text{HBr}$: The $\pi$-electrons attack $\text{H}^+$ (electrophile), forming a carbocation; then $\text{Br}^-$ (nucleophile) attacks the carbocation to form bromoethane.

principles.statement: Addition reactions are exothermic because the energy released forming two $\sigma$-bonds exceeds the energy required to break one $\pi$-bond and one reagent bond.
principles.application: This thermodynamic favorability explains why addition reactions proceed spontaneously once initiated. Typical $\pi$-bond energy is $\sim 264 \text{ kJ/mol}$; $\sigma$-bond energies are $\sim 350-435 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Net energy release drives the reaction forward.
principles.analogy: Think of it like a financial transaction: you 'spend' energy to break bonds (cost), but 'earn' more energy when new bonds form (profit). Addition reactions are 'profitable'—you earn more than you spend.
principles.example: Ethene hydrogenation: Break $\text{C}=\text{C}$ $\pi$-bond ($264 \text{ kJ/mol}$) + $\text{H}-\text{H}$ bond ($436 \text{ kJ/mol}$) = $700 \text{ kJ/mol}$ cost. Form two $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds ($2 \times 413 = 826 \text{ kJ/mol}$) = $826 \text{ kJ/mol}$ gain. Net: $-126 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (exothermic).

components.definition: Addition reactions involve three key components: (1) the alkene substrate with $\pi$-bond electrons, (2) the adding reagent ($\text{H}_2$, $\text{X}_2$, or $\text{HX}$), and (3) often a catalyst or specific conditions to facilitate the reaction.
components.interpretation: The reaction converts unsaturated alkenes ($sp^2$ carbons, planar geometry) to saturated products ($sp^3$ carbons, tetrahedral geometry). This changes molecular shape, physical properties, and reactivity. Regioselectivity and stereochemistry depend on the specific addition type.

image.alt: Diagram showing alkene, reagent, and product in addition reaction
image.caption: Addition components: alkene ($\pi$-bond) + reagent ($\text{A}-\text{B}$) $\rightarrow$ saturated product ($\text{C}-\text{A}$ and $\text{C}-\text{B}$ bonds).

sections.introduction: Hydrogenation is the addition of molecular hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) across the carbon-carbon double bond, converting alkenes to alkanes. This reaction requires a metal catalyst (Pt, Pd, or Ni) because the $\text{H}-\text{H}$ bond is too strong to break under normal conditions. Hydrogenation is industrially important for producing margarine from vegetable oils, refining petroleum, and synthesizing saturated compounds.

classifications.value: Requires metal catalyst (Pt, Pd, or Ni) to break $\text{H}-\text{H}$ bond and facilitate addition
characteristics: Syn addition (both H atoms add to same face of double bond)
classifications.behavior: The metal catalyst adsorbs both $\text{H}_2$ and alkene onto its surface. $\text{H}_2$ dissociates into atomic hydrogen on the metal surface. Hydrogen atoms transfer to the alkene sequentially, both from the same face (syn addition). The saturated product desorbs from the catalyst surface.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}} \text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_3$. Ethene (gas) converts to ethane (gas). Used industrially to remove alkenes from petroleum streams.
Unsaturated fatty acids (with $\text{C}=\text{C}$ bonds) in vegetable oils are hydrogenated to produce saturated or partially saturated fats (margarine). Controls melting point and shelf life.

classifications.value: Both hydrogen atoms add to the same face of the double bond
characteristics: Produces specific stereoisomers from cyclic alkenes
Important for stereochemical control in synthesis
classifications.behavior: Because both H atoms transfer from the catalyst surface to the same face of the alkene, the addition is syn. For cyclic alkenes, this produces cis products. For acyclic alkenes with substituents, specific stereoisomers form based on which face the catalyst approaches.

examples.explanation: Cyclohexene + $\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}}$ cyclohexane. Both H atoms add to the same face, but product is achiral (all positions equivalent in cyclohexane).

image.caption: Cyclohexene hydrogenation: syn addition produces cyclohexane.

examples.explanation: 1,2-Dimethylcyclohexene + $\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}}$ cis-1,2-dimethylcyclohexane. Both methyl groups end up on same side due to syn addition.

sections.key_concept: Hydrogenation: $\text{C}=\text{C} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Pd/Ni}} \text{C}-\text{C}$. Requires metal catalyst; syn stereochemistry; exothermic; industrially important for food and petroleum.

sections.introduction: Halogenation is the addition of molecular halogens ($\text{Cl}_2$ or $\text{Br}_2$) across the carbon-carbon double bond, producing vicinal dihalides (halogens on adjacent carbons). This reaction proceeds rapidly at room temperature without a catalyst and is useful for both synthesis and as a test for unsaturation (bromine water test). The mechanism involves a cyclic halonium ion intermediate, which controls stereochemistry.

categories.description: Addition of $\text{Br}_2$ or $\text{Cl}_2$ to alkenes produces vicinal dibromides or dichlorides
categories.detailed_mechanism: The reaction proceeds through a cyclic halonium ion intermediate. First, the $\pi$-electrons attack the halogen molecule, forming a three-membered cyclic halonium ion and expelling halide ion. Then, halide ion attacks from the back side (anti to the halonium bridge), opening the ring and forming the vicinal dihalide with anti stereochemistry.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{Br}_2 \rightarrow \text{BrCH}_2-\text{CH}_2\text{Br}$ (1,2-dibromoethane). Reaction is rapid at room temperature; red-brown $\text{Br}_2$ color disappears, indicating positive test for unsaturation.

Adding bromine water (orange-brown) to an unknown compound: if color disappears rapidly, compound contains $\text{C}=\text{C}$ or $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$. If color persists, compound is saturated. Classic qualitative test for unsaturation.

categories.description: Halogenation proceeds with anti addition (halogens add to opposite faces) due to cyclic halonium ion intermediate
categories.detailed_mechanism: The cyclic halonium ion blocks one face of the molecule. The halide ion must attack from the opposite (back) side, similar to $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ mechanism. This results in anti addition: the two halogen atoms end up on opposite faces of the original double bond. For cyclic alkenes, this produces trans products.

examples.explanation: Cyclohexene + $\text{Br}_2 \rightarrow$ trans-1,2-dibromocyclohexane. The two Br atoms add to opposite faces, producing trans stereochemistry. This is diagnostic for the halonium ion mechanism.

image.alt: Anti addition to cyclohexene showing trans product
image.caption: Cyclohexene bromination: anti addition produces trans-1,2-dibromocyclohexane.

examples.explanation: Step 1: $\pi$-electrons attack $\text{Br}_2$, forming cyclic bromonium ion + $\text{Br}^-$. Step 2: $\text{Br}^-$ attacks from back side, opening ring. Overall: anti addition.

sections.key_concept: Halogenation: $\text{C}=\text{C} + \text{X}_2 \rightarrow \text{X}-\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{X}$. Proceeds via cyclic halonium ion; anti stereochemistry; bromine water test for unsaturation.

sections.introduction: Hydrohalogenation is the addition of hydrogen halides ($\text{HCl}$, $\text{HBr}$, or $\text{HI}$) across the carbon-carbon double bond, producing alkyl halides. This reaction follows Markovnikov's rule for regioselectivity: the hydrogen adds to the carbon with more hydrogens, and the halogen adds to the carbon with fewer hydrogens (more substituted carbon). The mechanism involves a carbocation intermediate, which explains both regioselectivity and potential rearrangements.

principles.statement: In the addition of $\text{HX}$ to an unsymmetrical alkene, the hydrogen atom bonds to the carbon with more hydrogen substituents, and the halogen bonds to the carbon with fewer hydrogen substituents (more substituted carbon).
principles.application: This rule predicts the major product for hydrohalogenation of unsymmetrical alkenes. It arises from carbocation stability: the more stable carbocation forms preferentially (tertiary > secondary > primary), and the halogen adds to that carbon.
principles.analogy: Think of it as 'the rich get richer': the carbon that already has more carbons attached (more substituted) gets the halogen, while the carbon with more hydrogens gets the hydrogen. The more substituted carbocation is more stable, like a person with more friends for support.
principles.example: Propene + $\text{HBr}$: Major product is 2-bromopropane (Br on secondary carbon), not 1-bromopropane (Br on primary carbon). The secondary carbocation intermediate is more stable than primary.

principles.statement: The regioselectivity of hydrohalogenation is determined by the relative stability of carbocation intermediates: $3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ > \text{methyl}$.
principles.application: When $\text{H}^+$ adds to the alkene, two possible carbocations can form. The more stable carbocation forms faster and leads to the major product. This explains Markovnikov's rule mechanistically. Carbocation rearrangements can occur if a more stable carbocation is accessible.
principles.analogy: Carbocations are like 'electron-deficient people': tertiary carbocations have three carbon 'friends' to share electron density (hyperconjugation), making them more stable. Primary carbocations have only one 'friend', making them less stable and less likely to form.
principles.example: 2-Methylpropene + $\text{HCl}$: Forms tertiary carbocation (not primary), leading to tert-butyl chloride (2-chloro-2-methylpropane) as the major product.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Product for Propene + $\text{HBr}$
worked_examples.problem: Predict the major product when propene reacts with $\text{HBr}$. Explain using Markovnikov's rule and carbocation stability.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the alkene structure and possible addition sites.
Apply Markovnikov's rule: H adds to carbon with more H's.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{H adds to C1 (2 H's)} \rightarrow \text{Br adds to C2 (1 H)}$

step_by_step.explanation: Consider carbocation stability: two possible carbocations.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Option A: Primary carbocation (C1}^+) \quad \text{Option B: Secondary carbocation (C2}^+)$

step_by_step.explanation: Select more stable carbocation and form product.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Secondary carbocation more stable} \rightarrow \text{2-bromopropane is major product}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Major product: 2-bromopropane ($\text{CH}_3-\text{CHBr}-\text{CH}_3$). Markovnikov addition: H adds to C1 (more H's), Br adds to C2 (more substituted). Secondary carbocation intermediate is more stable than primary, explaining the regioselectivity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Markovnikov's rule: H adds to carbon with more H's; halogen adds to more substituted carbon. Carbocation stability ($2^\circ > 1^\circ$) determines major product.

worked_examples.title: Carbocation Rearrangement in Hydrohalogenation
worked_examples.problem: Predict the product when 3,3-dimethyl-1-butene reacts with $\text{HCl}$. Consider possible carbocation rearrangements.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw the alkene structure and identify initial carbocation.
Check for possible rearrangement to more stable carbocation.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Adjacent C3 is tertiary} \rightarrow \text{methyl shift possible} \rightarrow \text{tertiary carbocation}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Major product: 2-chloro-2,3-dimethylbutane (not 3-chloro-2,2-dimethylbutane). Carbocation rearrangement occurs: secondary carbocation rearranges to more stable tertiary carbocation via methyl shift before chloride addition.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Carbocation rearrangements: if a more stable carbocation is accessible via hydride or alkyl shift, rearrangement occurs before nucleophile addition. This can change the expected product structure.

sections.section_title: Mechanism Comparison: Three Addition Reaction Types
sections.introduction: While all three addition reactions convert alkenes to saturated products, their mechanisms differ significantly. Understanding these mechanistic differences explains the observed regioselectivity, stereochemistry, and reactivity patterns. This section compares the three mechanisms side-by-side.

process_flow.mechanism: $\text{H}_2$ adsorbs on metal surface and dissociates to atomic H. Alkene adsorbs on surface. H atoms transfer sequentially to same face of alkene. Product desorbs from surface.
process_flow.result: Syn addition; requires catalyst (Pt, Pd, Ni); exothermic; no carbocation intermediate

process_flow.description: Electrophilic addition via cyclic halonium ion
process_flow.mechanism: $\pi$-electrons attack $\text{X}_2$, forming cyclic halonium ion + $\text{X}^-$. Halide ion attacks from back side, opening ring. Anti addition results from back-side attack.
process_flow.result: Anti addition; no catalyst required; rapid at room temperature; cyclic intermediate prevents rearrangement

process_flow.mechanism: $\pi$-electrons attack $\text{H}^+$, forming carbocation. Halide ion attacks carbocation. Regioselectivity determined by carbocation stability (Markovnikov's rule).
process_flow.result: Markovnikov regioselectivity; carbocation can rearrange; no stereospecificity (planar carbocation)

sections.key_concept: Mechanism comparison: Hydrogenation (syn, catalyst, surface); Halogenation (anti, halonium ion, no rearrangement); Hydrohalogenation (Markovnikov, carbocation, rearrangement possible).

sections.introduction: The stereochemistry of addition reactions—whether atoms add to the same face (syn) or opposite faces (anti)—is determined by the reaction mechanism. Understanding stereochemical outcomes is crucial for predicting product structures, especially for cyclic alkenes and chiral molecules.

categories.description: Both atoms add to the same face of the double bond
categories.detailed_mechanism: In catalytic hydrogenation, both H atoms transfer from the metal catalyst surface to the same face of the alkene. This is enforced by the surface mechanism: the alkene lies flat on the catalyst, and H atoms approach from the surface side only.

examples.explanation: 1,2-Dimethylcyclohexene + $\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}}$ cis-1,2-dimethylcyclohexane. Both methyl groups end up on same side due to syn addition.

image.caption: Syn hydrogenation: both H atoms add to same face, producing cis product.

categories.description: Atoms add to opposite faces of the double bond
categories.detailed_mechanism: The cyclic halonium ion intermediate blocks one face of the molecule. The halide ion must attack from the opposite (back) side, similar to $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ inversion. This enforces anti stereochemistry.

examples.explanation: Cyclopentene + $\text{Br}_2 \rightarrow$ trans-1,2-dibromocyclopentane. The two Br atoms add to opposite faces, producing trans stereochemistry exclusively.

image.caption: Anti bromination: Br atoms add to opposite faces, producing trans product.

categories.description: No specific stereochemistry due to planar carbocation intermediate
categories.detailed_mechanism: The carbocation intermediate is planar ($sp^2$ hybridized), allowing the halide ion to attack from either face with equal probability. This produces racemic mixtures (equal amounts of both enantiomers) when chiral centers form.

examples.explanation: 1-Butene + $\text{HBr} \rightarrow$ racemic 2-bromobutane. The carbocation is planar; $\text{Br}^-$ attacks from either face, producing equal amounts of (R) and (S) enantiomers.

sections.key_concept: Stereochemistry: Hydrogenation = syn (same face); Halogenation = anti (opposite faces); Hydrohalogenation = non-stereospecific (planar carbocation, racemic mixtures).

sections.introduction: Addition reactions of alkenes have widespread applications in industry and the laboratory. From food production to pharmaceutical synthesis, these reactions are fundamental tools for chemical transformation. Understanding applications provides context for why these reactions are important beyond academic chemistry.

components.function: Converts liquid oils to semi-solid fats for food applications
components.key_feature: Controls melting point and texture; extends shelf life; partial hydrogenation creates trans fats (health concern); modern processes minimize trans fat formation

components.function: Removes reactive alkenes from fuel streams
components.key_feature: Improves fuel stability; prevents gum formation; enhances combustion properties; large-scale industrial process

components.function: Produces alkyl halides for further synthetic transformations
components.key_feature: Alkyl halides are versatile intermediates for $\text{S}_\text{N}1/\text{S}_\text{N}2$, Grignard, and elimination reactions; Markovnikov selectivity is useful for regiocontrol
Orange-brown color disappears if $\text{C}=\text{C}$ or $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ present; rapid, simple, diagnostic test; standard laboratory procedure

components.function: Produces dihalides for elimination reactions and other transformations
components.key_feature: Vicinal dihalides can undergo double elimination to form alkynes; anti stereochemistry is predictable and useful

components.function: Builds complex drug molecules from simpler precursors
components.key_feature: Stereochemical control is critical for drug activity; hydrogenation sets stereocenters; hydrohalogenation introduces functional handles for further modification

sections.introduction: Students often make predictable errors when learning addition reactions of alkenes. Understanding these pitfalls helps ensure accurate mechanistic understanding and correct prediction of reaction outcomes.

categories.type: Mistake: Confusing Syn and Anti Addition
categories.description: Mixing up which reactions give syn vs. anti stereochemistry
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students often assume all additions are syn or all are anti. In reality, hydrogenation is syn (surface catalysis), halogenation is anti (halonium ion), and hydrohalogenation is non-stereospecific (carbocation). The mechanism determines stereochemistry.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'Cyclohexene + $\text{Br}_2$ gives cis-1,2-dibromocyclohexane.' CORRECT: Cyclohexene + $\text{Br}_2$ gives trans-1,2-dibromocyclohexane (anti addition). Hydrogenation would give cis product (syn addition).

categories.description: Adding H and X to wrong carbons in hydrohalogenation
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students sometimes add H to the more substituted carbon (opposite of Markovnikov's rule). Remember: H adds to carbon with MORE H's already; X adds to carbon with FEWER H's (more substituted). Think 'H goes where H's are'.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 1-bromopropane (Br on primary carbon). CORRECT: 2-bromopropane (Br on secondary carbon). H adds to C1 (2 H's), Br adds to C2 (1 H, more substituted).
examples.equation: $\text{Markovnikov: H to more H's; X to more substituted}$

categories.description: Predicting products without considering possible carbocation rearrangements
categories.detailed_mechanism: In hydrohalogenation, if a more stable carbocation is accessible via hydride or alkyl shift, rearrangement occurs before halide addition. Students often predict the 'expected' Markovnikov product without checking for rearrangement possibilities.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 3-chloro-2,2-dimethylbutane (no rearrangement). CORRECT: 2-chloro-2,3-dimethylbutane (methyl shift to form tertiary carbocation). Always check for rearrangement when carbocations form.
examples.equation: $\text{Check: can carbocation rearrange to more stable form?}$

categories.description: Omitting catalyst requirement for hydrogenation reactions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Hydrogenation requires a metal catalyst (Pt, Pd, or Ni) because the $\text{H}-\text{H}$ bond is too strong ($436 \text{ kJ/mol}$) to break under normal conditions. Students sometimes write hydrogenation without specifying the catalyst, which is mechanistically incorrect.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3$ (no catalyst specified). CORRECT: $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3$. Catalyst is essential for reaction to occur.
examples.equation: $\text{Hydrogenation requires Pt, Pd, or Ni catalyst}$

categories.description: Mixing up addition reactions (alkenes) with substitution reactions (alkanes)
categories.detailed_mechanism: Addition reactions break $\pi$-bonds and add atoms (alkenes); substitution reactions replace atoms (alkanes). Students sometimes apply free radical substitution mechanisms to alkenes or addition mechanisms to alkanes.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'Ethene + $\text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{UV}}$ chloroethane + $\text{HCl}$' (substitution). CORRECT: Ethene + $\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow$ 1,2-dichloroethane (addition, no UV needed). Alkenes undergo addition; alkanes undergo substitution.

sections.introduction: Apply your understanding of addition reactions to these practice problems. Work through product prediction, mechanism writing, and stereochemistry determination to build proficiency.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Products for Multiple Addition Reactions
worked_examples.problem: Predict the major product for each reaction: (a) 2-Methylpropene + $\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}}$, (b) 2-Methylpropene + $\text{Br}_2$, (c) 2-Methylpropene + $\text{HBr}$.
worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 2-Methylpropane (hydrogenation, syn); (b) 1,2-Dibromo-2-methylpropane (bromination, anti); (c) 2-Bromo-2-methylpropane (hydrohalogenation, Markovnikov). Each reaction type gives different products based on mechanism.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Different addition reactions give different products: hydrogenation adds H-H; halogenation adds X-X; hydrohalogenation adds H-X with Markovnikov selectivity.

worked_examples.title: Determining Stereochemistry for Cyclic Alkene Addition
worked_examples.problem: Draw the product(s) with correct stereochemistry when 1-methylcyclohexene reacts with: (a) $\text{H}_2/\text{Pt}$, (b) $\text{Br}_2$.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze reaction (a): hydrogenation of cyclic alkene.

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Syn addition} \rightarrow \text{H atoms add to same face} \rightarrow \text{methyl and H cis}$
$\text{Product (a): cis-1-methylcyclohexane (methyl and new H on same side)}$

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze reaction (b): bromination of cyclic alkene.

step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Anti addition} \rightarrow \text{Br atoms add to opposite faces} \rightarrow \text{trans dibromide}$

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) cis-1-Methylcyclohexane (syn hydrogenation); (b) trans-1,2-dibromo-1-methylcyclohexane (anti bromination). Cyclic alkenes clearly show stereochemical differences: syn gives cis, anti gives trans.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Stereochemistry in cyclic alkenes: syn addition (hydrogenation) produces cis products; anti addition (halogenation) produces trans products. Ring structure locks stereochemistry.

worked_examples.title: Carbocation Rearrangement Problem
worked_examples.problem: Predict the major product when 3-methyl-1-butene reacts with $\text{HCl}$. Consider possible carbocation rearrangements.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw the alkene structure and identify initial carbocation from Markovnikov addition.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Structure: } \text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2 \quad \text{H}^+ \text{ adds to C1} \rightarrow \text{secondary carbocation at C2}$

step_by_step.explanation: Check for possible rearrangement to more stable carbocation.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Adjacent C3 is tertiary} \rightarrow \text{hydride shift possible} \rightarrow \text{tertiary carbocation}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Major product: 2-chloro-2-methylbutane (not 2-chloro-3-methylbutane). Carbocation rearrangement occurs: secondary carbocation rearranges to more stable tertiary carbocation via hydride shift before chloride addition.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Carbocation rearrangements in hydrohalogenation: always check if hydride or alkyl shift can produce more stable carbocation. Tertiary > secondary > primary stability order drives rearrangement.

sections.introduction: A quick-reference checklist consolidates the key concepts for predicting and understanding addition reactions of alkenes.

components.key_feature: Hydrogenation requires catalyst; halogenation is rapid; hydrohalogenation follows Markovnikov

components.function: Predicts which carbon gets H vs. X
components.key_feature: H to carbon with more H's; X to more substituted carbon; carbocation stability determines outcome

components.function: Predicts spatial arrangement of added atoms
components.key_feature: Cyclic alkenes clearly show stereochemistry: syn = cis, anti = trans

components.composition: For hydrohalogenation, check if carbocation can rearrange
components.key_feature: Hydride or alkyl shift to form more stable carbocation before nucleophile addition

components.composition: Ensure catalyst is specified for hydrogenation; room temperature for halogenation
components.function: Confirms reaction will proceed as expected
components.key_feature: No catalyst = no hydrogenation; UV light not needed for alkene addition (unlike alkane substitution)

summary: Addition reactions of alkenes involve breaking the $\text{C}=\text{C}$ $\pi$-bond and adding atoms across the double bond, converting $sp^2$ carbons to $sp^3$. Three major types: (1) Hydrogenation ($\text{H}_2$ + catalyst): syn addition, converts alkenes to alkanes, industrially important for food and petroleum; (2) Halogenation ($\text{X}_2$): anti addition via cyclic halonium ion, produces vicinal dihalides, bromine water test for unsaturation; (3) Hydrohalogenation ($\text{HX}$): Markovnikov regioselectivity via carbocation intermediate, produces alkyl halides, carbocation rearrangements possible. Key principles: $\pi$-bond is nucleophilic (attacks electrophiles); addition is exothermic (two $\sigma$-bonds form, one $\pi$-bond breaks); regioselectivity determined by carbocation stability ($3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ$); stereochemistry determined by mechanism (syn for hydrogenation, anti for halogenation, non-specific for hydrohalogenation). Common mistakes: confusing syn/anti stereochemistry, misapplying Markovnikov's rule, forgetting carbocation rearrangements, omitting catalyst for hydrogenation, and confusing addition (alkenes) with substitution (alkanes). Mastery requires practice predicting products, understanding mechanisms, and recognizing stereochemical outcomes for different reaction types.
